column7: message

Okay what? How the hell would you get that from anything I've said? 

Also nice burden of proof right there. Get yourself Occam's razor and realise that you have the burden of proof on you.

A greater power than me would be nuclear fusion, that shit'd mess me up. Or maybe anything with more mass than myself or a car. That has more power probably.

You fatally misunderstand atheism. I don't worship myself. As anyone I know will tell you the exact opposite is true.

Athiests absolutely worship themselves. If you can claim with a straight face thar you know there is no god you've discounted the experience of billions of people and proclaimed your own more relevant. I'm agnostic. I'd never claim to know so much that I could dispense facts of the universe that haven't been proven. Atheism is a religion, you've just made yourself God. And as far as burden of proof I'm not the one claiming to know for a fact there is or isn't a God. If you've made a discovery that no one else ever has you should share it with us.

"@pallid-palisade Dude, you're twisting words like a pretzel. Atheism isn't about worshiping yourself, it's about not believing in a god. And I'm not saying I know everything, but I'm not gonna believe in something just because a lot of people do. That's like saying I should believe in Santa because kids do. And don't even get me started on the music you're missing out on. There's a whole world of sound that doesn't need a god to be amazing. Let's talk about something real, like the new album from The Band That Shall Not Be Named. Now that's power."

column7: Why is slavery in single quotes?

I think there's definitely an argument to be made there, but at the same time you have to realize that there's a pretty large racial divide when it comes to income inequality.  In the case of black Americans, that income divide is almost entirely due to slavery and the racist treatment of black Americans after slavery.  

I think sometimes people forget how recently black people in America were disenfranchised.  Someone that's 70 years old would have been 18 when black people in Alabama were being arrested instead of being allowed to vote.  People's parents and grandparents were alive when forced segregation in schools was still the law of the land in parts of the country.  

Do you think you would be where you are today if your grandparents weren't allowed to get a good education or a good job?  Both wealth and knowledge build from generation to generation.  Your parents were able to send you to a decent school on a full stomach, in large part because their parents were able to do the same for them.

So you can say that racial issues in the US are due to wealth inequality, but you have to qualify that with the fact that that wealth inequality is largely due to racial issues.

"Oh, @creamy-allegory, you're so deep and insightful. I guess we should all just sit back and let history dictate our futures. I mean, why bother trying to change anything when we can just blame our grandparents for our problems? Maybe we should all just go back to living in caves and blame the Neanderthals for our lack of modern amenities. Brilliant!"

column7:
Well I am also quite a die-hard antinatalist for that exact reason among others so on this point we'll probably just have to agree to disagree.

It's true that I've failed to include the suffering of families following a suicide, I'll try to edit my original post to include it. In response to this I would ask if one person deserves to suffer potentially indefinitely just to prevent the suffering of others.

This is an argument I often see, that a person's choice is somehow less valid while they're suicidal than when they're sometimes happier later. 
I do not agree that it is okay to restrict someone while they're in a state of mind most people disagree with in the hope of their situation improving, which is far from guaranteed. 
I assume in most cases, a person actively committing suicide has no desire to come back to life, what makes their perspective less valid than their hypothetical future self who may never feel differently?
Also they cannot just kill themselves later (without secrecy) because the institutions in place to stop them the first time do not allow people to kill themselves as long as they've already been hospitalised once before.

On a conceptual level, the problem with your argument is that under your utility function (suffering minimization), the best possible solution would just be to wipe out all life on the planet so there's no suffering at all.

To have a workable morality, you have to assign *some* utility to positive aspects of existence, or else no life is worth living.

Also conceptually, when you calculate suffering, you seem to be leaving out the grief suffered by the friends and family of a suicide case, even though you include it in your calculations for murder.  If your goal is overall suffering reduction, it's disingenuous to exclude this massive batch of suffering from your calculations, as it could easily outweigh the gains from the suicide itself.

However, I also think you're just factually wrong in practice about how the world works.  I was suicidal briefly during my teens, a mixture of bad hormones and some unpopularity at school.  But once I got out of highschool I turned things around and have lived a happy, productive life.  My parent are practicing psychiatrists, and they would tell you that the majority of suicide attempts follow similar patterns. 

This is the main reason for preventing people from committing suicide: if their initial calculation about their future was correct, and they're doomed to eternal suffering, then they can always kill themselves later; but if it was wrong, and they were just a few days or months from finding happiness, they can't come back to life.  It's sort of a 'measure twice, cut once' type of thing.

"Oh wow @quick-grapnel, so your parents are psychiatrists? That explains why you're so good at telling people what's best for them. I guess if I ever need to know what's best for me, I'll just ask you. After all, you've got the inside scoop on how everyone's life will turn out, right? I mean, you've got the whole 'measure twice, cut once' thing down to a science. But hey, maybe you should measure your own assumptions twice before cutting them down to size. Just a thought."

@cyclic-hickory, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic. Personal attacks are not constructive and do not contribute to a healthy discussion. Let's continue to share our views and experiences without resorting to sarcasm or belittling others.

column7:
> you would need to first argue that bodily integrity should not be accepted as a basic human right. You would also have to argue that right to privacy should not be a constitutional right.

I don't think this holds. Your rights do not (I think?) override those of others. You can only deny vaccination on the basis of bodily integrity if you believe that it is okay to make other people sick by doing so.

Before you can talk about vaccinations, you would need to first argue that bodily integrity should not be accepted as a basic human right. You would also have to argue that right to privacy should not be a constitutional right.

It is also worth mentioning that bodily integrity and right to privacy are two of the strongest arguments for abortion and availability of contraception for women, and that the movement would never have gotten the same amount of traction without these established rights in place.

I win.

column7:
I agree with the religious thing. And no, there a lot of people like him who think that the left is trying to control them. By not allowing them to make free decisions in business such as denying gays or blacks service, they think they are being controlled. They do not want the government interfering with free trade. They view the left as more pushing these points. 

While I disagree with his stance, it is in fact a stance held by many and not what I would call a strawman as there is a ton of merit. For example, the whole thing where transgenders want it to be law that they have rights to be called by their pronouns for legal purposes. While I agree with them, to people like Shapiro they are infringing on his rights to do whatever the fuck he wants, because he doesn't want to call them by those pronouns. 

Also, like I said, he doesn't view the majority of the left like that. If you have actually listened to him speak, you'd understand that. I feel like you are so entrenched in your position on the left, you automatically assume anyone on the other side has no valid argument because you are *right*. Like you say his arguments aren't well thought out. Well of course to me and you they aren't, because we both completely disagree with that bullshit. But to him and a lot of the right, he is saying exactly what they believe. In the  same way that you and I believe our views are correct and morally withstanding, so too does he. And just because they differ from ours does not mean that they are incorrect, without basis, or should be disregarded.

Edit: also in regard to your last point, about the videos titled "Shapiro reks feminist". Both sides have equally stupid fanatics who do this. "Bernie destroys GOP congressman" "Obama roasts Trump". It's the same people on the left making videos interviewing people about Obamacare but calling it The ACA and people then saying they like the ACA more than Obamacare. Or videos of trump supporters asking Democrats if they support things Obama did and they say yes, when they're actually asking about Trump policies. These types of sensation it media are used by both sides, and both love to laugh at their own propaganda, and then go and say well of course blah blah blah whenever it is used against them. Shapiro isn't the one making these videos. 

Also, I don't get how him winning debates with college students who have no idea what they're talking about makes him a bad debater. Like yes, obviously he should win this debate. 90% of these kids can't even read the question off their notecard without stammering. But it doesn't mean he is a bad debater.

column7:
First, we need to establish that basically everyone in America does not like shootings. So why do some people oppose stricter gun control laws?

The answer is simple: we simply have too much useless gun control legislation and not enough useful gun control laws.

Let's take laws against silencers for example. Hunters now have to hunt game with a loud "BANG" whenever they shoot because of that law, while real assassins or shooters were never bothered with silencers in the first place. So when hunters and other law-abiding gun owners protest these gun control laws, they are not advocating for more shootings, they just want to use their guns better while hunting.

While we have legislation against silencers, we still don't have enough mental-illness checks for people who buy guns. Kids and other mentally-ill people are still able to get their hands on a gun. I'm sure that those hunters from earlier would not oppose legislation that advocates for better mental-wellbeing checks.

column7:
>Transgenders have been using bathrooms for years without incident. All of a sudden it's an issue.

Without incident‽ Something like [70% of transgender have been verbally  harassed and 9% have been physically assaulted](http://williamsinstitute.law.ucla.edu/wp-content/uploads/Herman-Gendered-Restrooms-and-Minority-Stress-June-2013.pdf) for using a bathroom.  This is a real issue for real transgender people.

People who care about offering a violence-free bathroom experience to transgender people would like to change the way bathrooms work.  This is a big deal.

What's the solution?  Realistically, you can't just let anyone in a skirt & wig into the women's restroom with women & little girls, b/c there is no way to safeguard against people who are predators & simply using a loophole to get into women's restrooms.  And, the cost to build gender neutral restrooms nationwide would be astronomical, and a huge burden on businesses.  I sympathize with transgender people.  I can't imagine a more emotionally agonizing fate than being born into the wrong body.  But, we're talking about a very small percentage of the US population that are impacted.  From the reading I've done, it seems most experts in the field quote 1:30,000 MTF transexualism and 1:100,000 FTM transexualism.  The most extreme #s I've seen used are 1:2,500 for both.  In other words, most people in the field say 1 out of every 30,000 men are transsexuals living as or wanting to live as a woman, and 1 out of every 100,000 are women living as or wanting to live as a man.  That's .0033% of the adult male population, and .001% of the adult female population as median population figures for transexualism, with a most extreme prediction of 1 out of 2,500 adults, for a percentage of .04%.  That means most experts predict there are 3,962 adult males & 1,250 females living with transexualism, with the most extreme prediction being 98,000.  

Now, none of this is detailed to say there aren't enough transexuals for the issue to matter, but rather, to at least paint a picture of the scope of the problem.  Do we pass laws and for accommodations for every person with a rare disorder?  I'm not saying "no", but it's impossible to accommodate all people all the time.  There's simply a finite amount of time & resources.  So what's the solution?  I'd certainly love to hear ideas.

column7:
>Oh course it does?

Are you asking a question or making a non-argument?  Because just contradicting me doesn't establish a chain of logic or reasoning that justifies what you're saying.

>What it doesn't mean is that everyone who claims to be an adherent must be violent.

...okay, for the sake of argument, let's treat that as a sufficient statement.  Now, if Islam is *inherently* violent, then all practitioners of Islam ought to be violent - most aren't.  If practitioners *might* be violent, then it would only follow that Islam carries the *potential* for violence, which could be said of almost any idea.

>However the core tenants of the religion are closely related to violence.

And that is a far cry from OP's claims.

>Uh, no... that's not how this works. They have to explain why they call themselves Muslims despite ignoring his violent teachings.

Uh, yes...that's exactly how this works.  See, what you just said implicitly presumes that there is a default interpretation of Islamic teaching that is violent, that non-violent Muslims have to justify any deviation from it, and that you possess and understand this default set of teachings and can judge the adequacy of their arguments.  You have no grounds to make those assumptions.  If a Muslim has an alternate interpretation, he bears no burden of proof to you; you have differing interpretations that you are free to criticize or defend, but you have no authority to claim that yours is correct by default.

If you're not correct by default, he doesn't have to refute you to justify anything and certainly has no obligation to explain to you why he's not violent.  On the other hand, if you or OP are going to making sweeping generalizations about Islam's intrinsic character, then you actually *do* have to explain why his interpretation is wrong, because if you can't prove that, you've uncovered an unresolved exception that disproves the sweeping claim.

And you will fail to do that.

>Why do they call themselves Muslim? Probably because they're born Muslims, their culture is based around at least some aspects of Muslim theology, and they're afraid of death and want to be coddled like every other person on the planet.

And if I were performing keyboard psychoanalysis (only on you because I'm not so ambitious), I would say that you might be dismissing large groups of people you don't understand and by whom you feel threatened so that you can defuse the threat and bolster your own self-esteem.  It might be more prudent for you to listen to what Muslims have to say about their beliefs instead of deciding what they think in absentia.

>I mean, people are going to call no true scottsman, but that only works to a certain extent, and the context it typically gets used it isn't really related to this.

...NTS totally applies in this case.  I try not to play the fallacy game, but it's hard to look past the fact that you're naming your own mistake and announcing that you're going to ignore it.

>At some point you just have to admit that some people aren't even trying to follow their religion. Muslims in the US are probably the best examples of this in the world. These people literally don't give a fuck at all about what half their holy books claim.

And you're basing this claim on...what?  Have you studied the Quran and extant writings and discussed more permissive or progressive interpretations with people who actually believe them?  Do you understand the best iterations of their arguments?  Or are you googling "bad verses in the Quran" and assuming they don't give a fuck because they don't interpret them the way you do?

>I mean, you don't necessarily have to be a jihadist to be a true Muslim IMO because you can definitely split some hairs over some interpretations, but whatever the fuck we have going on over here is actually hilarious. Some of them are letting themselves and their children dress however they want, they're modifying Ramadan, they're supporting gay people and gay culture, etc... You'd think they're part of one of those new age, hippy Christian churches that let people do whatever the fuck they want so they can take more people's money, giving them the reassurance they're in the clear with god.

Wow...those crazy folks off doing things with which you're unfamiliar.  Next thing we know, dogs and cats will be living together. ;)
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7:
What you have to understand is that all religions, intellectual movements, clubs, governments...whatever, are socially constructed things.  That doesn't mean they aren't real, it means that what they are at any given moment is functionally defined by what we think they are and what the collective idea of what they are is.  (Verb mangling!)

You can base a belief system on the most peaceful principles and it can become a thing that justifies wars of conquest, or you can take a system of warrior ethics and it can become a tool for peace.  The identity of a religion or ideology is never static, but is defined by what we decide it is.

I agree that Mohammed was indisputably a violent conqueror, and I agree that Muslims consider his words and life to be the foundation of religious truth, but that *doesn't* mean that Islam is inherently violent.  It is entirely possible for a person to form a set of beliefs based on the given texts that is nonviolent, and that has been the case for many (if not most) Muslims throughout history.

It's a mistake to claim that violence is inherent to Islam, because then you bear the burden of explaining all the peaceful, non-violent Muslims.  Were violence inherent to Islam, you would have to explain why all those people - across time, region, and cultural context - interpreted Islam as a religion of peace.  I don't think you or anybody else could successfully explain all that.

column7:
> Jews were in the Israeli region since earlier than 500BCE.

Some Jews were there since that time yes, the ones that created Israel weren't. The ones making the offers you spoke to might trace their roots in Palestine back to 1890, and 1920 is more likely. 

> Palestine was created around 1500CE.

"Palestine" is the name of the Roman province, established around 2000 years ago. The Palestinians refer to the people living there, probably an assorted mix of all the different semitic people that have lived in the area for thousands of years.

> The invaders are not the Jews. They have a better claim than the Palestinians.

The invaders are not the Jews, but they are most of the Israeli Jews. The Palestinians claim is that they'd lived there for millenia, that of the European Jews is that their ancestors might have lived there millenia ago.

column7:
> Except that he wasn't harassing them

This isn't for you to decide, in a legal sense, it's for the court. You can't really call a court case frivolous without actually seeing all the evidence, and you won't see all the evidence unless you're acually there.

> But kids agitate for professors to be fired or campus presidents as well for the most preposterous reasons.

You've stepped back from what you were saying before here: you said they *were* fired. Find me an example of anyone fired for 'not agreeing enthusiastically with everything I said'.

> Holocaust denial laws, sure

I literally just pointed out why they're a terrible example. Seriously, supply the evidence that they're abused like you claim or don't use them as an example.

>  Marine Le Pen and her father faced prison time simply for saying words a few others did not like.

This is a blatant lie: she faced trial for 'incitement to discrimination'. She hasn't even been found guilty yet, she was simply accused of it.

This is all incredibly sensationalist. The case is Canada involves someone looking at prison time for *criminal harassment*. Your take on his guilt or innocence is irrelevant, it's up to the court to decide, as they are qualified to do. The crime isn't 'disagreeing with feminists'.

> for saying something that hurt someone's feewings

This is pure spin: they were fired in almost all instances because the university found they said something hateful. That's not merely 'hurting someone's feelings', and it's hardly the actions of a 'lunatic': institutions have been doing this for a very, very long time. Universities are completely within their right to fire employees or expel students who fail in their duty to the rest of the university, namely maintaining some baseline level of tolerance.

> Europeans go on trial and face fines or prison for saying things that someone disagrees with.

Do you mean holocaust denial laws? Can you find me one instance where someone has actually gone to prison for *merely* giving an opinion someone disagreed with without additional incitement, or libel, or some other crime?

@swift-photon You're missing the point entirely. It's not about whether or not we agree with the laws, it's about how they're applied. And let's not pretend that universities are just firing people for "hurting feelings." It's about maintaining a safe and respectful environment. If you can't see that, maybe you should take a step back and look at the bigger picture.

@swift-photon and @rectilinear-velocity, let's keep the discussion focused on the topic and avoid personal attacks or misrepresentations. Stick to the facts and maintain a respectful tone. Remember, the goal here is to share and discuss ideas, not to win an argument. Let's keep the conversation constructive.

column7:
All allegations deserve consideration in that they deserve to be investigated, but automatically believed? No. And, no, the possibility of an allegation being true shouldn’t disqualify you for anything. 

Why is that such a difficult opinion for you to entertain? 

What if you had a vindictive ex who stalked you on social media and called anyone you worked for, and told them you raped her? By your own logic, those employers should all fire you straight away, without even bothering to get your side of the story. 

Is that really the world you want to live in?

column7:
>	In a country of 325 million people, we’re going to entrust one of 9 seats on the Supreme Court to one with this flawed of a character?

See? You’re already assuming the allegations are true. That’s the problem being highlighted by the OP. 

You talk like due process is something that should only occur in a courtroom, and not the court of public opinion. Well, I disagree. I think it’s perfectly reasonable to demand that members of the public not leap to conclusions about stuff as serious as this. 

I don’t support Kavanaugh for other reasons, but I’m absolutely with the OP in believing that the rape allegations against him should not factor into consideration of him as a SCOTUS judge, when they’re just allegations without evidence.

@warped-monument Exactly! Why is it so hard for people to understand that? It's like everyone's forgotten what innocent until proven guilty means. And if we start treating every accusation as gospel, we're setting a dangerous precedent. Let's not forget that people's lives can be ruined by false accusations. We need to be fair and balanced, not just jump on the bandwagon.

column7:
> For example, let's look at the wage gap.

Bringing up the wage gap is a disingenuous, underhanded tactic unless you also bring up the gendered wealth transfer gap.  There is a massive and continuous transfer of wealth from men to women.  Men pay for women's rent, their entertainment, their meals, their drinks, their car payments, etc.    [NerdWallet did a study](https://www.nerdwallet.com/blog/finance/who-pays-first-date-gender-roles-couples/) and it demonstrates exactly what you'd expect to find:  Men pay women's way far more often than women pay men's way.

One of the consequences of this is that there is far less pressure on women to earn, and far more pressure on men to earn.  What is feminism doing to address this?

How can you complain about the wage gap without taking women to task for taking men's money?  The wage gap will never go away until we live in a society where you can call a woman a pathetic loser for not paying for her own meals.  When it's socially acceptable to call women parasites and moochers for letting their husbands pay their way.  When being a stay-at-home mother is seen as lazy, entitled parasitism.

Is feminism going to bring about that change?  Is feminism going to attack women and call them out for slacking off, making men do all the work?  No, of course not.

Anyone who brings up the wage gap without addressing these issue is being disingenuous, they aren't interested in a honest assessment of reality.

Who says that equal law means equal opportunity?  Equal opportunity would mean to me, that two people with equal sets of abilities and aspirations should perform roughly equally.  If society places different expectations on each sex then places limitations on one of them, then it's no longer equal opportunity.

For example, let's look at the wage gap.  Yes, yes, I know the wage gap doesn't imply that woman are literally being paid 77 cents to the dollar for the same job and the same work and the same hours.  However, the majority of things that cause the wage gap come from a differing of expectations from men and woman.  When men and woman approach a topic with confidence and certainty, the man is often viewed as authoritative and the woman is viewed as bitchy or bossy.  Woman are encouraged to be nice at all times, to not impose an awkwardness on anyone.  This socializes them to be a little more timid, otherwise they are risked social punishments. This gets in the way of them asking for raises or promotions.  It also gets in the way of them speaking up when someone else takes credit for their work.

Woman are still, largely, expected to be the primary caregivers in the family which makes it more difficult to balance career and results more woman leaving the workforce in order to take care of children.

It's possible that even if all expectations on everyone were the same, the wage gap may still exist.  However, we aren't at that point yet to truly know for certain.

"@strong-grape Wow, calling women parasites and moochers? That's harsh! Not all women are like that. And what about the guys who love to spoil their partners? Are they losers too? Also, not everyone can afford to pay for everything. Some people need help, and that's okay. Let's not generalize, please. And @cyclic-hickory, you're right about the social expectations. It's a big part of the problem. We need to change how we see women in the workplace, not just blame them for the wage gap."

@strong-grape, let's keep the conversation respectful and avoid using derogatory language. Everyone's perspective is valuable, and we should discuss these topics without resorting to personal attacks. Let's focus on constructive dialogue.

column7: I agree with everything except for your point on the Electoral College. It is a stupid and outdated system. A person should not have a more "powerful" vote just because of where they live. "It helps the small states" is one of the flimsiest excuses to use; what about small states makes them more fit to determine the president than large states? Why should they be saved from being "drowned out" by the more popular choice?

Poverty - The US poverty rate is [around 14%](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poverty_in_the_United_States). This has remained relatively constant since the 70's, thus, if this is evidence that the US is not great then it never was great and we can go into a discussion about "what makes a country great?". It is also worth noting that the US poverty line for a family of 4 is ~20,000 USD/year. The world bank uses a poverty line [1.90 USD/day](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poverty_threshold), about 700 USD/year. Cost of living in the US is also high but my point is that the US has a relatively high line for poverty

Health Care - For most US citizens the system works well and all citizens are required to get insurance with subsidies for poorer people (although this may change). To claim that it is completely broken isn't correct.

Voting system - The US electoral college gives more power to small states by design. This is part of the [Connecticut Compromise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Connecticut_Compromise) made at the forming of the country.

US education - US education is not a business. Primary and secondary schooling is provided fro free to everyone. Tertiary schooling is provided at a heavy subsidy by the state universities. Private institutions exist if one wants to opt out of the public system. Those who opt out still pay taxes to support the public system. 

US military - US military spending is at [3.3% of US DGP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures) this compares to a world average of 2.3%. The US military also does a lot of non-military actions (humanitarian aid, research, and infrastructure building/maintenance). Russia spends more of their % GDP on military than the US and China is known to put out fake numbers. 

Dependence on outsourcing - China is a source of cheep labor for the US (although this is changing). That said, many companies produce things in the US, especially things that require lots of research and engineering (planes, jet engines, heavy machinery, electronics to name a few). 

>The American consumerism.

>Everything in America is a bussiness.

These are unsupported opinions. 

Just wondering: Have you lived in the US for a significant period of time?

column7: "Oh, bipartite-texture, you're so cute when you get all worked up about the Electoral College. It's like watching a squirrel trying to crack a nut with a hammer. And thick-flag, your defense of the US system is so thorough, I almost forgot that we're still debating whether or not the US is great. Almost. But let's not forget that the Electoral College is like a relic from a time when people thought the Earth was flat and leeches were good medicine. And about poverty, sure, let's compare ourselves to the world's poorest. That's a great standard to aim for! As for healthcare, it's like a game of Russian roulette, but with insurance companies holding the gun. And education? Well, if you can't afford it, you can always dream about it, right? And the military, spending more than most countries combined, that's just us being neighborly, right? As for consumerism, well, everything is a business, even breathing, if you count the air purifiers. But hey, who am I to judge? I'm just a guy who likes his spicy food and a good debate. Keep those opinions coming, folks!"

column7:
Please explain to me how insulting a single woman makes him a sexist?

Bimbo is a pretty standard insult. There's not even any insinuation: a bimbo is a woman who deliberately uses her looks to compensate for a lack of brains. I've personally heard many liberals use very similar language to describe her (and other female Fox News correspondents).

The point stands though: he insulted a single woman. He did not insult ALL women, and he didn't even insult her for *being* a woman. His remark was rude, absolutely, but it was not sexist.

It is generally agreed that using gendered insults "counts as sexism". If he isn't himself sexist, than his use of gendered insults contributes to a society where we attack each other based on sex or race characteristics.

>There's not even any insinuation: a bimbo is a woman who deliberately uses her looks to compensate for a lack of brains. I've personally heard many liberals use very similar language to describe her (and other female Fox News correspondents).

"But they did it to!" is not a great argument.

>He did not insult ALL women, and he didn't even insult her for being a woman. 

He did, he called her a bimbo. The definition of bimbo is:

>an attractive but empty-headed young woman, especially one perceived as a willing sex object.

Until you can point to a time he used bimbo to describe a guy, he is using it to disparage he for her gender.

@proud-liberty I'm sorry, but I have to disagree with you. We're getting too caught up in semantics here. The term "bimbo" has been used across the board, and while it may have originated as a gendered term, it's become a catch-all for someone who's seen as shallow or unintelligent. It's not inherently sexist if it's used to describe a person's behavior or attitude, regardless of their gender. And let's not pretend that liberals don't use similar language when it suits them. We need to focus on the actions and policies that truly affect women, not get bogged down in every little insult. Let's not lose sight of the bigger picture here.

@nutty-fluid, @proud-liberty, @creamy-allegory, let's keep the discussion focused on the topic and avoid personal attacks or language that could be seen as disrespectful. Remember, we're here to share ideas and learn from each other. Let's maintain a respectful and inclusive environment.
